label: Fundamentals
heroTitle: Learn the basic ideas that appear in almost every system design interview.
heroDescription: Start here if system design feels overwhelming. These topics explain what good systems are trying to achieve and why engineers make trade-offs.
cardIntro: Open any concept to study it in much more depth with simple explanations and examples.

items.title: Latency and Throughput
items.summary: Latency is how long one request takes. Throughput is how much work the system finishes over time.
quickPoints: Low latency means a single request feels fast.
High throughput means the system can handle many requests.
A system can be fast for one user but still fail under heavy traffic.
items.whatItMeans: Latency is the waiting time for one request. Throughput is the number of requests, jobs, or messages handled in one second or one minute.
items.whyItMatters: Interviews often ask you to make a system feel fast while also supporting growth. You need to explain whether you are solving a speed problem for one user or a scale problem for many users at once.
items.simpleExample: If a food stall serves one customer in 30 seconds, that is latency. If it serves 120 customers in one hour, that is throughput.
realWorldExamples: A search bar needs low latency because users notice even small delays.
A log processing pipeline needs high throughput because it must handle a huge number of events.
A payment service needs both: each payment should be quick, and the service should stay stable during traffic spikes.
items.interviewTip: Say clearly whether the product cares more about fast single responses, large overall capacity, or both.
tradeOffs: Batching can increase throughput, but one item may wait longer before it is processed.
Heavy caching can reduce latency, but stale data may appear.
More worker machines can increase throughput, but coordination becomes harder.
commonMistakes: Using latency and throughput as if they mean the same thing.
Optimizing one slow endpoint without checking if the real bottleneck is total traffic.
Ignoring network delay, database delay, and queue delay when estimating latency.

sections.title: How to think about it
paragraphs: Latency answers the question, "How long will one user wait?" Throughput answers the question, "How much total work can the system finish?"
When traffic grows, a system may still look fast in tests with a few users, but real users can suffer when the total load becomes too high.
bullets: User-facing products usually care a lot about latency.
Background systems often care a lot about throughput.
Many systems need a balance between the two.

sections.title: Interview example
paragraphs: Suppose you are designing a photo upload service. Upload completion time matters to the user, so latency matters. At the same time, the backend must process many uploads in parallel, so throughput matters too.
bullets: Use queues to smooth traffic spikes.
Use multiple workers for image processing.
Use a CDN so later reads stay fast.

items.title: Availability
items.summary: Availability means the system is up and usable when people need it.
quickPoints: Highly available systems keep working even when some parts fail.
Redundancy helps, but it increases cost.
You should explain what level of downtime the product can tolerate.
items.whatItMeans: Availability is the chance that a user can successfully use the system when they try. It does not mean the system is perfect, only that it remains reachable and useful.
items.whyItMatters: Different products need different uptime levels. A banking app and a casual blog do not need the same failure tolerance.
items.simpleExample: If one grocery store counter closes but other counters keep working, customers can still check out. That is similar to keeping extra servers ready when one fails.
realWorldExamples: A ride-hailing app should keep booking rides during peak time even if one server crashes.
A video app may keep streaming with slightly lower quality instead of going fully down.
An internal analytics tool may accept some downtime if cost matters more than perfect uptime.
items.interviewTip: Always ask how much downtime is acceptable. That changes how much replication, failover, and cross-region support you need.
tradeOffs: More replicas improve availability, but increase cost and data synchronization work.
Cross-region deployment improves resilience, but adds complexity and consistency challenges.
Fast failover improves uptime, but may require more infrastructure kept ready all the time.
commonMistakes: Saying a system is highly available without explaining how failures are handled.
Ignoring single points of failure like one database or one load balancer.
Forgetting that maintenance and bad deployments can also hurt availability.

sloPack.title: Availability targets in production
sloPack.intro: Senior answers usually turn availability into a measurable target instead of a vague wish.
indicators: P95 latency on the critical path
targets: A product-specific success-rate target such as 99.9 or 99.95 percent
A latency target tied to user expectations
A stricter target for checkout or auth than for internal dashboards
errorBudgetPolicies: Slow risky launches when the budget is being burned too quickly
Prefer rollback or traffic shift before deep debugging during active incidents
Separate the most critical user journey from lower-priority features
interviewMoves: Tie the target to business harm
Explain what the team does when reliability slips
sloPack.closing: Availability becomes a senior discussion when it is connected to a target and an operating policy.

incidentRunbook.title: Availability incident flow
incidentRunbook.intro: High availability is not only about extra replicas. It is also about how the team responds when those replicas are not enough.

stages.title: Confirm blast radius
stages.description: Check whether the issue is local to one service, one zone, or a wider region.

stages.title: Reduce user harm
stages.description: Fail over, shed non-critical work, or route traffic away from the bad path.

stages.title: Validate recovery
stages.description: Watch success rate, latency, and dependency health before declaring the system healthy.

immediateActions: Protect the critical user journey first
Avoid making multiple risky changes during the same incident
postmortemActions: Identify whether a bad deployment, dependency failure, or capacity gap started the incident
incidentRunbook.closing: Availability is partly an architecture property and partly an operating habit.

relatedTopics.title: SLIs, SLOs, SLAs, and Error Budgets

sections.title: What usually improves availability
paragraphs: You usually improve availability by adding redundancy and by isolating failures. If one machine, zone, or region fails, another should take over.
bullets: Multiple app servers behind a load balancer.
Health checks and automatic traffic shifting.

sections.title: What to say in interviews
paragraphs: If the interviewer asks for a globally used product, mention how traffic can be routed away from a bad region. If the product is smaller, explain that a single region with replicas may be enough at first.
bullets: Show how reliability grows with scale.
Link the design to business importance.

items.title: Consistency
items.summary: Consistency is about whether users see the latest correct data when they read from the system.
quickPoints: Strong consistency gives the newest data right away.
Eventual consistency allows a short delay before all copies match.
The right choice depends on the product, not on buzzwords.
items.whatItMeans: If one user changes data and another user reads it, consistency decides how fresh and correct that read will be.
items.whyItMatters: Some systems must show the latest value immediately. Others can tolerate a short delay if that makes scaling easier.
items.simpleExample: If you change your profile picture and one device still shows the old one for a few seconds, that is eventual consistency.
realWorldExamples: Bank transfers usually need stronger consistency so balances stay correct.
Social feed likes can often tolerate small delays.
Shared document editing may need special conflict handling instead of simple strong or eventual consistency.
items.interviewTip: Tie the consistency model to user harm. Ask what goes wrong if a user sees old data for a few seconds.
tradeOffs: Strong consistency improves correctness, but may add latency.
Eventual consistency scales well, but users may briefly see stale data.
Conflict resolution logic helps disconnected systems, but makes product behavior harder to explain.
commonMistakes: Choosing strong consistency everywhere even when the product does not need it.
Saying eventual consistency is always okay because it scales better.
Ignoring what users see during replication lag.

goodVsWeakAnswer.question: How should you talk about consistency in an interview?
goodVsWeakAnswer.weakAnswer: I would use eventual consistency because it scales better.
goodVsWeakAnswer.goodAnswer: I would choose the consistency model based on user harm. For balances and payments, I would prefer stronger guarantees. For likes and feed counters, brief staleness may be acceptable.
goodVsWeakAnswer.whyGoodWorks: The stronger answer ties the decision to product correctness instead of buzzwords.

seniorConsiderations: Different features in the same product may need different consistency levels.
Read-after-write expectations should be called out explicitly.
Replica lag and conflict resolution should be discussed in production terms.
multiRegionNotes: Cross-region replication can improve resilience, but increases write latency and conflict handling complexity.
Some data may stay regional while user-facing summaries are eventually synced globally.
observabilityNotes: Track replica lag, stale-read complaints, and conflicting-write incidents.
Measure how often user flows are blocked by stronger consistency choices.

sections.title: Strong vs eventual
paragraphs: Strong consistency is useful when wrong data would break trust or business logic. Eventual consistency is useful when small temporary differences are acceptable.
bullets: Inventory: depends on how strict overselling rules are.

sections.title: Simple way to explain it
paragraphs: Do not overcomplicate this in interviews. Just explain how long users can safely wait before all parts of the system show the same answer.

sections.title: Production consistency decisions
paragraphs: Senior answers often go one step further and say which paths need read-after-write guarantees, which paths can tolerate stale data, and what the user sees during lag or conflict.
bullets: Mention whether one writer owns each entity.
Explain how stale data is surfaced or hidden from users.

relatedTopics.title: Quorum, Leader Election, and Split Brain

items.title: Partitioning and Sharding
items.summary: Partitioning splits data across many machines so one machine does not hold everything.
quickPoints: Sharding helps when one database is too large or too busy.
Good shard keys spread traffic evenly.
Poor shard keys create hot spots.
items.whatItMeans: Instead of storing all data on one machine, you divide it into pieces and place those pieces on different machines.
items.whyItMatters: Large systems outgrow one database in storage, read traffic, write traffic, or all three.
items.simpleExample: A library may store books in different rooms based on category. That makes it easier to scale, but if one room gets all the popular books, that room becomes crowded.
realWorldExamples: A messaging app may shard by conversation ID so different chats spread across servers.
A ride service may shard by region because most trips stay within one city.
A social app may shard by user ID, but celebrity users can still create hot spots.
items.interviewTip: Do not just say 'use sharding'. Explain the key, why it spreads load, and what happens when traffic is uneven.
tradeOffs: Sharding improves scale, but cross-shard queries become harder.
Resharding helps future growth, but moving live data is risky.
Range-based sharding can support ordered queries, but hot ranges are possible.
commonMistakes: Choosing a shard key without thinking about real traffic patterns.
Ignoring rebalancing when a shard grows too hot.
Forgetting that joins and transactions are harder across shards.

migrationPlaybook.title: Re-sharding playbook
migrationPlaybook.intro: Re-sharding is not just moving data. It is moving data while live traffic keeps flowing.

phases.title: Choose a safer target key or layout
phases.description: Decide the new shard strategy based on the real hot-spot pattern, not guesswork.

phases.title: Copy gradually
phases.description: Move data in controlled batches while the live system still works.

phases.title: Cut reads and writes over carefully
phases.description: Shift traffic a little at a time and compare hot-key behavior before full cutover.

risks: Backfill traffic hurting the live database
Old code still assuming the previous shard key
One hot entity staying hot even after re-sharding
migrationPlaybook.closing: A good senior answer says how the system changes safely, not only that it will be re-sharded someday.

seniorConsiderations: Hot partitions matter more than average distribution.
Re-sharding needs live-migration thinking, not just a new diagram.
Entity ownership and cross-shard transactions should be kept as simple as possible.

sections.title: Common sharding approaches
paragraphs: Hash-based sharding spreads data fairly well. Range-based sharding is easier for ordered data. Geo-based sharding is helpful when users mostly stay in one region.
bullets: Geo: good for local traffic and compliance.

sections.title: How to explain hot spots
paragraphs: A hot spot happens when one shard gets much more traffic than others. This often comes from poor key choice or from a few very popular users.
bullets: Split hot entities further if needed.

items.title: Replication
items.summary: Replication means keeping the same data in more than one place so the system can survive failures and serve more reads.
quickPoints: Replication creates extra copies of data.
It helps availability and read scale.
It can also create lag and conflict problems.
items.whatItMeans: Instead of storing one copy of data on one machine, the system keeps multiple copies on different machines or in different zones.
items.whyItMatters: If one machine fails and the only copy of data is lost, the service may stop working. Replication lowers that risk.
items.simpleExample: If you keep one notebook at home and another in your bag, you can still work even if one is missing. That is the basic idea behind replication.
realWorldExamples: Ecommerce product catalogs use replicas for heavy reads.
Payment systems keep multiple copies so failures do not lose transactions.
Global apps replicate data to be closer to users in different regions.
items.interviewTip: Explain whether replication is mainly for failover, read scaling, geographic reach, or all three.
tradeOffs: More replicas improve resilience, but increase synchronization work.
Async replication is faster, but replicas may be stale.
Sync replication improves correctness, but can add write latency.
commonMistakes: Using the word replication without saying how fresh replicas are.
Assuming every replica is safe for read-after-write traffic.
Treating replication like a backup strategy.
whatInterviewerExpects: A clear explanation of why extra copies exist.
Awareness that replicas can lag behind.
A good reason for choosing sync or async replication.
followUpQuestions: What happens if the primary node fails?
Can users read from replicas right after a write?
How would you replicate across regions?

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Explain replication choices for a global ecommerce catalog.

seniorConsiderations: Replication strategy should match the write path and failure model.
Failover plans are not complete without safe promotion and recovery steps.
Replication does not remove the need for backup and recovery planning.
multiRegionNotes: Global replication reduces distance to users, but cross-region writes may need clear ownership rules.
Some systems use one write region per entity to reduce conflict complexity.
observabilityNotes: Track replication lag, failed replication events, and failover recovery time.
Alert when read traffic is hitting stale replicas on sensitive paths.

migrationPlaybook.title: Safe failover and promotion
migrationPlaybook.intro: Replication becomes a senior topic when you explain how promotion stays safe under failure instead of only saying a replica takes over.

phases.title: Detect primary failure carefully
phases.description: Avoid promoting a new leader after a short network glitch that may still leave the old primary active.

phases.title: Promote one clear writer
phases.description: Use a safe ownership rule so two nodes do not both accept writes.

phases.title: Rebuild lagging replicas
phases.description: Make sure followers catch up and stale clients move to the new leader safely.

risks: Promoting a replica that is missing critical writes
Old clients still writing to the former primary
migrationPlaybook.closing: Replication is easy to draw and hard to fail over safely. That is the senior difference.

relatedTopics.title: Quorum, Leader Election, and Split Brain

sections.title: Primary and replica
paragraphs: A common pattern is one primary node for writes and one or more replicas for reads and failover.
bullets: Failover promotes a replica when needed.

sections.title: What senior interviewers expect next
paragraphs: After that baseline explanation, strong answers discuss promotion safety, stale clients, and whether majority agreement or other leader-election rules protect the write path.
bullets: How do you avoid two active leaders?
How do clients learn where writes go now?

items.title: CAP Theorem
items.summary: CAP is a simple way to talk about what a distributed system does during network trouble.
quickPoints: CAP matters only when there is a network partition.
You usually choose between stronger consistency and staying fully available.
The best choice depends on product needs.
items.whatItMeans: If network links break between parts of a distributed system, the system cannot fully keep both perfect consistency and full availability at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Interviewers use CAP to see whether you understand trade-offs in real distributed systems, not to hear textbook words only.
items.simpleExample: Imagine two store branches cannot talk to each other for a while. They can either pause risky actions until they sync again, or keep serving customers even though numbers may not match for a short time.
realWorldExamples: A bank may pause or limit some operations when correctness matters most.
A social feed may keep working and accept small delays in freshness.
A shopping cart may stay available while stock counts catch up later.
items.interviewTip: Explain CAP with user impact. Say what users see during the partition and why that choice is acceptable.
tradeOffs: Choosing consistency may block some operations during failures.
Choosing availability may show old or conflicting data for a while.
Real systems often mix choices for different features.
commonMistakes: Explaining CAP as if all three parts are always competing all the time.
Using CAP without tying it to an actual product decision.
Thinking one system must use the same choice for every feature.
whatInterviewerExpects: A product-based reason for the consistency or availability choice.
Examples of which data can be stale and which cannot.
followUpQuestions: Which parts of this product can tolerate stale data?
Would you choose the same CAP trade-off for payments and likes?
How would you explain replica lag to users?

goodVsWeakAnswer.question: What is a strong CAP explanation?
goodVsWeakAnswer.weakAnswer: CAP means you can only choose two out of three things.
goodVsWeakAnswer.goodAnswer: CAP becomes relevant during network partitions. In that moment, the system usually chooses whether to prefer stronger consistency or keep serving requests with temporary data differences.
goodVsWeakAnswer.whyGoodWorks: The stronger answer explains when CAP matters and how it affects product behavior.

seniorConsiderations: CAP choices can differ by feature inside one product.
Production systems often mix synchronous and asynchronous paths to balance user experience and correctness.
Partition behavior should be explained in terms of user-visible consequences.

sections.title: Simple way to remember it
paragraphs: During a network partition, you choose whether to wait for agreement or keep serving requests with the risk of temporary differences.

items.title: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
items.summary: Vertical scaling makes one machine stronger. Horizontal scaling adds more machines.
quickPoints: Vertical scaling is simple at first.
Horizontal scaling helps growth and resilience.
Horizontal scaling also adds coordination work.
items.whatItMeans: Vertical scaling means adding more CPU, memory, or storage to one machine. Horizontal scaling means adding more machines and spreading work across them.
items.whyItMatters: This is one of the first scaling decisions you will discuss in interviews.
items.simpleExample: If one cashier is too slow, you can either give that cashier a faster machine or open more checkout counters.
realWorldExamples: A small internal tool may scale vertically for a long time.
A social app with large traffic often needs horizontal app servers.
A growing search service may need both stronger nodes and more nodes.
items.interviewTip: Start simple: say what can stay on one stronger machine early on, then explain when adding more machines becomes necessary.
tradeOffs: Vertical scaling is easier to operate, but has hard limits.
Horizontal scaling improves resilience, but requires load balancing and distributed state handling.
Not every database or workload scales horizontally in the same easy way.
commonMistakes: Assuming horizontal scaling is always the right first step.
Ignoring the cost and complexity of spreading state.
Forgetting that some systems can scale one layer horizontally but not another.

goodVsWeakAnswer.question: How should you compare vertical and horizontal scaling?
goodVsWeakAnswer.weakAnswer: Horizontal scaling is better because big companies use it.
goodVsWeakAnswer.goodAnswer: Vertical scaling is often the simpler early move, but it has hard limits. Horizontal scaling is stronger for growth and resilience, but adds load balancing and distributed state complexity.
goodVsWeakAnswer.whyGoodWorks: The stronger answer shows trade-offs, timing, and practical evolution.

seniorConsiderations: A mature design often scales different layers differently.
Stateless services are easier to scale horizontally than stateful storage layers.
Operational complexity should rise only when the scale actually demands it.

sections.title: Good interview framing
paragraphs: A strong answer often says: start by scaling vertically while the product is small, then move to horizontal scaling as traffic and reliability needs grow.
bullets: Good for early speed of development.
Move to many machines when limits appear.
Explain what operational work grows after that.

items.title: Load Shedding
items.summary: Load shedding means dropping some work on purpose so the whole system does not collapse.
quickPoints: It protects the system during spikes.
Not every request is equally important.
A smaller graceful failure is better than a total outage.
items.whatItMeans: When traffic is too high, the system rejects or delays less important work so the most important paths stay alive.
items.whyItMatters: Many systems do not fail because of one bug. They fail because overload spreads everywhere. Load shedding limits the damage.
items.simpleExample: During a rush, a restaurant may stop taking custom special orders so it can still serve normal meals on time.
realWorldExamples: A search site may skip expensive suggestions during extreme load.
A video service may reduce recommendation updates but keep playback alive.
A payment service may block non-critical analytics while still processing payments.
items.interviewTip: Say which requests are most important and which can be delayed, simplified, or rejected.
tradeOffs: Dropping work protects the core service, but some features temporarily degrade.
Aggressive shedding improves stability, but may frustrate some users.
Soft degradation is better for user experience, but harder to design.
commonMistakes: Trying to save every request equally during overload.
Not defining priority classes before failure happens.
Ignoring user messaging when a request is rejected.
whatInterviewerExpects: A clear priority order for traffic.
An explanation of which features can degrade first.
Awareness that overload protection is part of reliability.

sections.title: Common shedding choices
paragraphs: Systems often shed optional work first: analytics, recommendations, background sync, or low-priority API calls.

items.title: Fault Tolerance
items.summary: Fault tolerance is the ability to keep working even when some parts fail.
quickPoints: Failures are normal in distributed systems.
The goal is not zero failure. The goal is surviving failure.
Isolation and fallback behavior matter.
items.whatItMeans: A fault-tolerant system expects machines, networks, or services to fail and is designed so those failures do not take everything down.
items.whyItMatters: Interviewers want to know whether you design only for the happy path or also for real production failures.
items.simpleExample: If one bus route is blocked but people can still reach work using another route, the transport network is more fault tolerant.
realWorldExamples: A service uses retries with backoff for short network failures.
A checkout flow has fallback payment providers.
A video app switches to lower quality instead of failing playback.
items.interviewTip: Name the likely failure points and say what the system does next instead of only saying 'we add retries.'
tradeOffs: Retries improve resilience, but can increase load during incidents.
Fallback systems improve uptime, but add operational cost.
Isolation improves stability, but increases architecture complexity.
commonMistakes: Relying on retries for every failure type.
Ignoring timeouts and circuit breakers.
Not separating critical from non-critical services.
followUpQuestions: What happens if a dependency becomes slow instead of fully down?
Which failures should trigger retries and which should not?
How would you isolate one noisy service from the rest?

failureScenarios.title: Retry storm during an outage
failureScenarios.issue: Clients and services all retry at once after a dependency slows down.
failureScenarios.strongResponse: Use timeouts, backoff, circuit breakers, and request priority so recovery is possible instead of making overload worse.

debuggingChecklist: Check queue buildup or dropped work
seniorConsiderations: Fault tolerance is as much about isolation and recovery as it is about retries.
Graceful degradation matters when a full fallback is not possible.
Backpressure and overload control are core parts of reliability at scale.
observabilityNotes: Track latency percentiles, retry counts, timeout rates, and circuit-breaker trips.
Watch for cascading failures caused by one slow dependency.

incidentRunbook.title: Fault-tolerance response flow
incidentRunbook.intro: The team response is part of fault tolerance because a good system still needs good mitigation during real failures.

stages.title: Contain the blast radius
stages.description: Stop retries, pause risky background work, or isolate the hot dependency before the failure spreads.

stages.title: Protect the critical path
stages.description: Keep the most important user flows alive with fallback modes, degraded responses, or priority rules.

stages.title: Recover with evidence
stages.description: Use metrics and queue state to confirm the system is recovering instead of guessing.

immediateActions: Prefer safe degradation over heroic risky fixes
Check retry storms and queue buildup first
Use circuit breakers and load-shedding controls early
communicationRules: Describe what is degraded and what still works
Be explicit when the system is in fallback mode
postmortemActions: Add missing backpressure or isolation controls
Tune timeouts, retries, and fallback defaults
incidentRunbook.closing: Senior fault-tolerance thinking includes the incident path, not only the steady-state architecture.

sections.title: Useful fault-tolerance tools
paragraphs: Common tools include timeouts, retries, circuit breakers, queues, replicas, and fallback modes.
bullets: Isolate failures with queues and service boundaries.

items.title: Idempotency
items.summary: Idempotency means the same request can be repeated without causing the same action to happen many times.
quickPoints: It protects systems from duplicate requests.
It is very important for payments, retries, and webhooks.
The same request should lead to the same safe result.
items.whatItMeans: If a client sends the same request again because of timeout or retry, the system should not accidentally create duplicate side effects.
items.whyItMatters: Networks fail, users double-click, and clients retry. Without idempotency, one real action can become two or three actions by mistake.
items.simpleExample: If you press the elevator button three times, the elevator should still come once, not three times.
realWorldExamples: A payment API uses idempotency keys so one charge is not created twice.
Webhook handlers ignore duplicate event deliveries.
Order creation APIs prevent the same cart from becoming multiple orders.
items.interviewTip: When you mention retries, also mention how you avoid duplicate side effects.
tradeOffs: Idempotency improves safety, but needs request tracking or unique keys.
Stored keys take space and expiration policy work.
Not every operation is naturally idempotent, so the design may need extra logic.
commonMistakes: Adding retries without duplicate protection.
Assuming unique database IDs alone solve the whole problem.
Ignoring expiration and reuse rules for idempotency keys.
whatInterviewerExpects: Awareness that retries can create duplicates.
A concrete plan such as idempotency keys or request deduplication.
Examples of where idempotency matters most.
followUpQuestions: How long would you store idempotency keys?
Would you use the same design for payments and notifications?
What if the first request succeeded but the response was lost?

timedPrompts.title: Prompt 1
timedPrompts.prompt: Explain how you would protect a payment API from duplicate charges during retries.
checkpoints: Explain lost responses and safe replay.

securityPrivacyNotes: Idempotency keys should not leak sensitive meaning or be easy to guess.
Stored request records should follow retention and privacy rules.
seniorConsiderations: Idempotency becomes more important when external dependencies are slow or unreliable.
It should be part of the API contract, not only an internal patch.
Out-of-order or replayed events should be handled together with duplicate retries.

sections.title: Where it shows up
paragraphs: Idempotency is especially important in systems that use retries, message delivery, and client-side resubmission.

sections.title: Exactly-once myths
paragraphs: In interviews, it is stronger to say you aim for safe at-least-once processing with idempotent handlers than to claim exactly-once behavior everywhere with no explanation.
bullets: Make state transitions safe to repeat.
Treat replay and retry as normal conditions.
